name: Kuruvungna Springs
image_caption: The Kuruvungna Springs sacred site on the UCLA campus, maintained by the Gabrielino-Tongva Tribe.
short_description: A sacred freshwater spring on the UCLA campus, once the heart of the Tongva village of Kuruvungna.
full_description: Long before the arrival of Europeans, the Tongva people — also called the Gabrielino — thrived in the Los Angeles Basin for thousands of years. At what is now the UCLA campus, a freshwater spring known as Kuruvungna (meaning 'a place where we are in the sun') was the center of a thriving village. The Tongva built dome-shaped homes called 'ap' from willow poles and tule reeds nearby, and relied on the spring for drinking water, bathing, and ceremony. This site is one of the few remaining places in Los Angeles where the land itself retains memory of Indigenous occupation. Today, a small garden and plaque mark the spring on the UCLA campus, maintained in partnership with the Gabrielino-Tongva Tribe.

events.title: Tongva Settlement of the Basin
events.content: The Tongva people settle the Los Angeles Basin, establishing villages near reliable water sources. Kuruvungna becomes a central gathering place.

events.title: Village Life at Kuruvungna
events.content: At its peak, the village near Kuruvungna Springs had a population of several hundred people. Tongva society was organized around clans, with a hereditary chief called a 'tomyaar' leading each village.

events.title: Spanish Contact and Disruption
events.content: The founding of Mission San Gabriel Arcángel 10 miles east brought dramatic changes. Spanish missionaries began drawing Tongva people into the mission system, disrupting village life at Kuruvungna.

name: Ballona Wetlands
short_description: One of the last remaining wetlands in Los Angeles County, once a vital resource for the Tongva and later a hub of trade.
full_description: The Ballona Wetlands stretch across 577 acres near present-day Marina del Rey and Playa del Rey. For the Tongva, wetlands like Ballona were essential — providing tule reeds for basket weaving, waterfowl for food, and access to the ocean for fishing and trade with other coastal peoples. The Tongva used tomol (plank canoes) to navigate the coast and trade with communities as far away as Catalina Island and the Channel Islands. Over the 20th century, more than 90% of the original wetlands were drained or developed. The remaining parcel was purchased by the state of California in 2003 and is being restored.

events.title: Tongva Use of Coastal Resources
events.content: Tongva communities around Ballona harvest tule, catch waterfowl, and launch tomol canoes for ocean fishing. The wetlands are a crossroads of food and trade.

events.title: Rancho La Ballona Established
events.content: Mexican authorities grant Rancho La Ballona to the Talamantes and Machado families, beginning large-scale cattle grazing that begins to alter the wetland ecosystem.

events.title: Draining and Development
events.content: Flood control projects and real estate development drain much of the original wetlands. The Hughes Aircraft Company later builds facilities on the land.

name: San Gabriel Valley Foothills
short_description: The foothills of the San Gabriel Mountains sheltered dozens of Tongva villages and served as the cultural heartland of the region.
full_description: The San Gabriel Valley foothills form a dramatic backdrop to the Los Angeles Basin, and for the Tongva people, they were home. Over two dozen Tongva villages dotted the valley, each near streams flowing from the mountains. The Tongva maintained sophisticated trading networks, exchanging goods like obsidian, shell beads (used as currency), dried fish, and plant medicines. The acorn — gathered from the many oak trees of the foothills — was the dietary staple, ground into flour using stone mortars. Seasonal movements followed the food: coastal areas in summer, foothill acorn groves in fall. The Tongva's intimate knowledge of this landscape took thousands of years to develop and was nearly erased within a generation of Spanish colonization.

events.title: Acorn Economy of the Foothills
events.content: Tongva communities develop sophisticated acorn processing techniques. Women use bedrock mortars to grind acorns into flour, which is leached of tannins and cooked into porridge or bread.

events.title: Trade Networks Flourish
events.content: The Tongva develop extensive trade routes connecting the coast, the basin, and the desert. Shell beads from the Channel Islands serve as a regional currency.

events.title: Portolá Expedition Passes Through
events.content: The Spanish Portolá expedition, the first overland European crossing of California, camps near the San Gabriel River. The soldiers and priests note large Tongva settlements and the richness of the land.

name: El Pueblo de Los Ángeles
short_description: The original Spanish colonial settlement founded in 1781, now preserved as Olvera Street in downtown LA.
full_description: On September 4, 1781, Governor Felipe de Neve led a group of 44 settlers — pobladores — from the mission at San Gabriel to found El Pueblo de la Reina de Los Ángeles. The founding population was racially diverse: the settlers were a mix of Indigenous, African, and Spanish ancestry, reflecting the complex demographics of New Spain's northern frontier. The pueblo was laid out around a central plaza, which survives today as the Plaza near Olvera Street. For decades it remained a small agricultural town, growing crops and cattle for the nearby missions and the presidio at Santa Barbara. By 1800, the pueblo had grown to around 315 residents. Olvera Street, now a tourist corridor celebrating Mexican heritage, preserves the oldest surviving structures in Los Angeles.

events.title: Founding of the Pueblo
events.content: Governor Felipe de Neve establishes El Pueblo de la Reina de Los Ángeles with 44 settlers. The founding pobladores come from Sonora and Sinaloa, with diverse Indigenous, African, and Spanish heritage.

events.title: Growth Under Spanish Rule
events.content: The pueblo grows to over 300 residents. Agriculture and cattle ranching dominate the economy, with forced Tongva labor playing a central but under-documented role.

events.title: Mexican Independence
events.content: Mexico wins independence from Spain, and California becomes a Mexican territory. The missions are eventually secularized, releasing mission lands and transforming the regional economy.

name: Mission San Gabriel Arcángel
short_description: Founded in 1771, Mission San Gabriel was the economic and spiritual center of Spanish California in the Los Angeles region.
full_description: Mission San Gabriel Arcángel was founded on September 8, 1771, by Franciscan priests Ángel Fernández de Somera and Pedro Benito Cambón. It became the wealthiest and most productive mission in Alta California, producing wine, tallow, hides, and grain on a massive scale. This prosperity was built on the coerced labor of thousands of Tongva people, who were baptized, renamed, and forced to live and work within the mission walls. Epidemic diseases — measles, smallpox, and typhoid — devastated the Tongva population. Estimates suggest the Indigenous population of the Los Angeles Basin fell by 75% or more within a century of Spanish contact. The mission church, rebuilt in the 1790s after earthquake damage, still stands today in the city of San Gabriel.

events.title: Mission Founded
events.content: Franciscan priests establish Mission San Gabriel near the Tongva village of Shevaanga. Within years, thousands of Tongva are baptized and absorbed into the mission labor system.

events.title: Mission at Peak Production
events.content: Mission San Gabriel becomes the most productive mission in California, with over 1,600 Indigenous neophytes and vast herds of cattle. Wine from its vineyards is traded across Alta California.

events.title: Secularization
events.content: The Mexican government secularizes the missions, transferring land to private ranchers. The Tongva neophytes are technically freed but left landless and impoverished.

name: Fort Moore Hill
short_description: A hilltop fortification built by U.S. forces in 1847, marking the transition from Mexican to American rule in Los Angeles.
full_description: Fort Moore Hill overlooks the original Plaza of Los Angeles. In July 1847, U.S. Army soldiers under Colonel Jonathan Stevenson constructed a fort here to secure American control following the Mexican-American War. The American flag was raised over Los Angeles on July 4, 1847, in a ceremony at the fort. Though the fort itself was short-lived, Fort Moore Hill represents a pivotal transition — Los Angeles shifting from a Mexican pueblo to an American city. A monument and relief panel now stand on the hill, near what is today the Los Angeles Civic Center, commemorating the event. The hill itself was partly excavated in the 1950s during freeway construction.

events.title: U.S. Forces Occupy Los Angeles
events.content: Commodore Robert Stockton leads U.S. forces into Los Angeles following the Battle of Río San Gabriel. The city changes hands from Mexico to the United States during the Mexican-American War.

events.title: Fort Moore Constructed
events.content: The U.S. Army builds Fort Moore on a hilltop overlooking the Plaza. The American flag is raised over Los Angeles for the first time on Independence Day.

events.title: Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo
events.content: The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo ends the Mexican-American War. California is ceded to the United States, and Los Angeles officially becomes an American city.

name: Old Chinatown
short_description: The original Chinese settlement near the Plaza, demolished in 1933 to build Union Station — a story of displacement and resilience.
full_description: Chinese immigrants began arriving in Los Angeles in the 1860s, initially brought to work on the transcontinental railroad. By the 1870s, a small Chinese community had formed near the Plaza, just blocks from where the town was founded. This neighborhood became known as Chinatown — a tight-knit community of laundries, restaurants, herb shops, and temples. In 1871, anti-Chinese violence erupted in what became known as the Chinese Massacre of 1871, one of the largest mass lynchings in American history, in which a white mob killed approximately 19 Chinese residents. Despite this, the community endured and grew. In 1933, however, the entire neighborhood was demolished to build Union Station. Residents were displaced and forced to rebuild their community several blocks north — the current Chinatown.

events.title: Chinese Immigrants Arrive
events.content: Chinese laborers, many recently arrived to work on the transcontinental railroad, begin settling near the Plaza in Los Angeles. They establish laundries and small businesses.

events.title: Chinese Massacre of 1871
events.content: A white mob attacks the Chinese neighborhood, killing approximately 19 residents. It remains one of the deadliest episodes of anti-Chinese violence in U.S. history.

events.title: Chinatown Demolished for Union Station
events.content: The city condemns the original Chinatown to build Union Station. Hundreds of Chinese residents and businesses are displaced. The community rebuilds several blocks north.

name: Bradbury Building
short_description: Built in 1893, the Bradbury Building is LA's oldest commercial landmark — an ornate symbol of the city's late 19th-century boom.
full_description: The Bradbury Building at 304 South Broadway was completed in 1893, commissioned by mining millionaire Lewis Bradbury and designed largely by draftsman George Wyman, who legend says was convinced to take the job via a Ouija board message from his deceased brother. The result is one of the most remarkable interiors in American architecture: a soaring five-story atrium filled with ornate ironwork, glazed yellow brick, and natural light pouring through a massive glass roof. It was built during Los Angeles's first great real estate boom of the 1880s, when the population of LA exploded from 11,000 to over 50,000 in a single decade, driven by the completion of the transcontinental railroad and aggressive land promotion. The Bradbury Building survived the boom-and-bust cycle and became a National Historic Landmark in 1977.

events.title: The Great Boom of the 1880s
events.content: The arrival of the Santa Fe Railroad in 1885 ignites a price war with the Southern Pacific, driving fares to as low as $1 from Missouri. Land prices soar; the population of Los Angeles explodes.

events.title: Bradbury Building Completed
events.content: The Bradbury Building opens on Broadway in downtown Los Angeles, showcasing the city's ambitions as an architectural and commercial center.

events.title: Broadway as the Commercial Hub
events.content: Broadway in downtown LA becomes the premier shopping and entertainment district west of the Mississippi, lined with grand theaters and department stores.

name: Hollenbeck Park
short_description: Donated to the city in 1892, Hollenbeck Park was Los Angeles's first public park and anchor of the Boyle Heights neighborhood.
full_description: Hollenbeck Park in the Boyle Heights neighborhood was donated to the City of Los Angeles in 1892 by Elizabeth Hollenbeck in memory of her husband, John Edward Hollenbeck. It became the first public park in Los Angeles and was a symbol of the city's growing civic ambitions. Boyle Heights, the neighborhood surrounding the park, became one of the most ethnically diverse communities in 20th-century America — home at various times to large communities of Jewish, Japanese, Mexican, Russian Molokans, and African American residents. The park's lake and green spaces were a gathering place for all of them. Today, Boyle Heights is predominantly Latino, and Hollenbeck Park remains a cherished community anchor, though the neighborhood has faced intense gentrification pressure in recent decades.

events.title: Park Donated to the City
events.content: Elizabeth Hollenbeck donates land to Los Angeles for a public park in memory of her husband. It becomes the city's first public green space.

events.title: Boyle Heights as Immigrant Hub
events.content: Boyle Heights becomes one of America's most diverse urban neighborhoods, home to Jewish, Japanese, Mexican, and Russian Molokan immigrants — many excluded from other LA neighborhoods by restrictive covenants.

events.title: Japanese American Incarceration
events.content: After the attack on Pearl Harbor, the Japanese American community of Boyle Heights is forcibly incarcerated. Many return after the war to find their homes and businesses gone.

name: La Placita Church (Plaza Catholic Church)
short_description: The oldest Catholic church in Los Angeles, founded in 1784 and a spiritual center for the city's Latino community for centuries.
full_description: La Placita — Our Lady Queen of Angels Church — sits beside the original Plaza of Los Angeles and has been at the heart of the city's Catholic and Latino community since it was founded in 1784. The current building, completed in 1822, is the oldest surviving church structure in Los Angeles. For generations of Mexican and Central American immigrants, La Placita has been a sanctuary — literally and figuratively. In the 1970s and 1980s, Father Luis Olivares made La Placita a prominent center of the Sanctuary Movement, openly harboring undocumented refugees from El Salvador and Guatemala who were fleeing civil wars. The church's history mirrors the broader story of Latino Los Angeles: resilience, faith, and advocacy across centuries.

events.title: Church Founded
events.content: The first chapel near the Plaza is established to serve the small colonial pueblo. It is staffed by Franciscan priests from Mission San Gabriel.

events.title: Current Building Completed
events.content: The present adobe church structure is completed, becoming the parish church of El Pueblo de Los Ángeles under Mexican rule.

events.title: Sanctuary Movement
events.content: Father Luis Olivares declares La Placita a sanctuary for undocumented refugees fleeing violence in Central America, drawing national attention and FBI scrutiny.

name: Watts Towers
short_description: Built solo by Italian immigrant Simon Rodia over 33 years, the Watts Towers are one of America's greatest works of folk art.
full_description: The Watts Towers — formally known as Nuestro Pueblo ('Our Town') — are a collection of 17 interconnected sculptural structures in the Watts neighborhood of South Los Angeles. Italian immigrant Simon Rodia built them entirely by himself, without machinery or assistants, from 1921 to 1954. He used steel pipes, wire mesh, and cement, adorning the surfaces with tens of thousands of pieces of broken tile, glass, pottery, and shells gathered from around the neighborhood. At their tallest, the towers reach 99 feet. Rodia never fully explained his motivations, but the towers became a symbol of the creative power of ordinary people. The Watts neighborhood became nationally known for a different reason in August 1965, when the Watts Uprising — sparked by a traffic stop — resulted in 34 deaths and over $40 million in damage, exposing the deep inequalities of life in South Los Angeles.

events.title: Rodia Begins Construction
events.content: Simon Rodia, a tile setter by trade, begins building his towers in the backyard of his Watts home. He works alone, nights and weekends, for 33 years.

events.title: Towers Completed and Donated
events.content: Rodia completes the towers and deeds his property to a neighbor before moving away. He never returns to see them again.

events.title: Watts Uprising
events.content: The Watts Uprising, triggered by a racially charged traffic stop, burns for six days. The towers survive untouched — neighbors protect them. The event forces a national reckoning with racism and urban poverty.

name: Griffith Observatory
short_description: Gifted to the city by Colonel Griffith J. Griffith in 1896, the park and observatory became one of LA's greatest public spaces.
full_description: Griffith Park — the largest municipal urban park in the United States — began as a gift from Colonel Griffith J. Griffith, who donated over 3,000 acres of his Rancho Los Feliz to the City of Los Angeles in 1896, intended as a gift to the common people. The Griffith Observatory, built on the park's southern slope, opened in 1935 and offered free public telescope viewing from the start. It sits on land that was once part of Rancho Los Feliz, granted to the Feliz family during the Mexican period, and later the site of a famous contested inheritance case. From the observatory, one can see the entire Los Angeles Basin — a view that collapses the city's entire history into a single panorama. The Griffith Observatory was famously featured in the 1955 film 'Rebel Without a Cause' with James Dean.

events.title: Griffith Donates the Land
events.content: Colonel Griffith J. Griffith donates 3,015 acres of Rancho Los Feliz to the City of Los Angeles, envisioning a park where working-class people can enjoy nature.

events.title: Observatory Opens
events.content: The Griffith Observatory opens to the public, offering free access to telescopes and planetarium shows. Over 10,000 people visit on opening day.

events.title: Hollywood Icon
events.content: James Dean's 'Rebel Without a Cause' films a pivotal scene at the observatory, cementing it as a symbol of Los Angeles youth culture and Hollywood mythology.

name: Chavez Ravine
short_description: A Mexican American community bulldozed for public housing, then given to the Dodgers — one of LA's most painful displacement stories.
full_description: Chavez Ravine, a hilly area just north of downtown Los Angeles, was home to a close-knit community of Mexican American families — the neighborhoods of Palo Verde, La Loma, and Bishop. In the late 1940s, the city began acquiring the land for a planned public housing project. Residents were told to sell their homes with promises of priority placement in the new housing. But the housing project was killed by anti-communist politics in 1952. The land sat cleared and empty. In 1958, the city gave the land to the Brooklyn Dodgers as an incentive to relocate from New York. The remaining holdout families were forcibly removed — some carried out by police in nationally televised scenes. Dodger Stadium opened in 1962 on the site of what had been a living community.

events.title: Community Displacement Begins
events.content: The City of Los Angeles acquires land in Chavez Ravine for a proposed public housing project. Over 1,000 families — mostly Mexican American — are displaced with promises of new housing.

events.title: Housing Project Cancelled
events.content: Amid Red Scare politics, the Los Angeles City Council cancels the public housing project. The cleared land sits vacant for years, and displaced families receive nothing.

events.title: Dodger Stadium Opens
events.content: Dodger Stadium opens on the land where the community once stood. The last holdout family, the Arechiga's, were forcibly evicted by sheriff's deputies in 1959 in scenes broadcast on local TV.

name: MacArthur Park
short_description: Once LA's premier park, MacArthur Park reflects the waves of immigration that reshaped the city throughout the 20th century.
full_description: Westlake Park — renamed MacArthur Park in 1942 to honor General Douglas MacArthur — opened in 1890 and was for decades one of the most fashionable destinations in Los Angeles, ringed by luxury hotels and frequented by the city's elite. By the mid-20th century, the neighborhoods around it shifted as middle-class white residents left for the suburbs. The park became a hub for successive waves of Central American immigrants: Guatemalans, Salvadorans, and Nicaraguans fleeing civil wars in the 1980s. Today, the area around MacArthur Park is one of the most densely populated neighborhoods in Los Angeles and a center of Latino immigrant life. The park gained national notoriety on May 1, 2007, when LAPD officers violently dispersed an immigration rights rally there, injuring journalists and protesters.

events.title: Westlake Park Opens
events.content: Westlake Park opens as a fashionable green space on the western edge of downtown. The artificial lake and boathouse attract wealthy Angelenos.

events.title: Central American Immigration Wave
events.content: Refugees from civil wars in El Salvador, Guatemala, and Nicaragua transform the neighborhoods around MacArthur Park into the heart of Central American Los Angeles.

events.title: May Day Police Crackdown
events.content: LAPD officers fire rubber bullets and beat journalists covering an immigration rights rally at MacArthur Park. The incident prompts the firing of the police chief and a national debate on policing.

name: The Hollywood Sign
short_description: Originally a 1923 real estate advertisement reading 'HOLLYWOODLAND,' the sign became the global icon of American entertainment.
full_description: The Hollywood Sign on Mount Lee in the Santa Monica Mountains began as a temporary real estate advertisement. In 1923, Los Angeles Times publisher Harry Chandler spent $21,000 to erect a giant sign reading 'HOLLYWOODLAND' to advertise a new housing development in the hills above Hollywood. The sign was built of telephone poles, pipes, wire, and 4,000 lightbulbs. It was never meant to be permanent. But as Hollywood became the center of the global film industry in the 1920s and 1930s, the sign took on a life of its own. The 'LAND' portion was removed in 1949. The sign fell into disrepair by the 1970s — one 'O' collapsed, and a letter 'H' toppled — before a celebrity fundraising campaign led by Alice Cooper and others restored it in 1978. The sign is now one of the most photographed landmarks in the world.

events.title: Sign Erected as Advertisement
events.content: The 'HOLLYWOODLAND' sign is built to advertise a real estate development. At 50 feet tall and 450 feet wide, it is visible across the Los Angeles Basin.

events.title: Hollywood Film Industry Dominates
events.content: Hollywood becomes the undisputed center of the global film industry. Studios like Paramount, MGM, and Warner Bros. produce the movies that shape global popular culture.

events.title: Sign Restored
events.content: A celebrity campaign raises $250,000 to restore the deteriorating sign. Each letter is sponsored by a celebrity — Alice Cooper paid for an 'O' in memory of Groucho Marx.
